site.title: auto-refine demo
site.tagline: Optimize bounded artifacts against a trusted evaluator — and keep only the changes that survive cost, latency, safety, and hype constraints.
site.proofNote: This page is generated from actual auto-refine run summaries plus a thin narrative metadata layer. Most examples are deterministic proof tasks, and the issue-triage benchmark is backed by public `cli/cli` issue excerpts with source URLs. The metrics, decisions, artifacts, and kept/discarded outcomes all come from real runs.

whatGetsOptimized.label: Bounded artifact
whatGetsOptimized.value: A file or policy you allow the system to change
whatGetsOptimized.detail: Examples: a prompt file, a routing policy, a scoring rubric, a config, or even repo-owned marketing copy.

whatGetsOptimized.label: Trusted evaluator
whatGetsOptimized.value: A command that produces measurable results
whatGetsOptimized.detail: The evaluator decides quality, latency, cost, safety, or any other metrics you care about.

whatGetsOptimized.label: Objective + constraints
whatGetsOptimized.value: Improve one thing without breaking the others
whatGetsOptimized.detail: Higher quality is not enough if the candidate is slower, pricier, unsafe, or overhyped.

whatGetsOptimized.label: Rollback-safe loop
whatGetsOptimized.value: Bad candidates are discarded and reverted
whatGetsOptimized.detail: The incumbent only advances on accepted improvements.

decisionRules.title: KEEP
decisionRules.body: A candidate is kept only when it improves the primary metric and still satisfies all hard constraints.

decisionRules.title: DISCARD
decisionRules.tag: too expensive / too slow / unsafe / too hypey
decisionRules.body: A candidate is rejected even if it looks stronger on the primary metric, when it violates cost, latency, safety, or credibility rules.

decisionRules.title: ROLLBACK
decisionRules.body: After a discard, the mutable artifact is restored to the last accepted incumbent. The bad trial does not linger.

examples.title: Optimize a support-system prompt
examples.problem: The starting prompt is too generic. It produces weak answers because it does not ask clarifying questions, does not emphasize safety, and does not guide step-by-step help.
examples.objectiveSummary: Maximize answer quality while keeping latency <= 2000ms, cost <= $0.02, safety_failures == 0.
examples.optimizedThing: The engine edits the prompt text itself — not the model — and evaluates whether the new prompt produces better behavior under constraints.
examples.whyKept: The winning candidate raises quality dramatically while staying under latency and cost limits and keeping safety_failures at zero.

caseStudies.label: User asks for account-recovery help
caseStudies.baselineBehavior: Gives generic support language with no clarifying question and no structured steps.
caseStudies.bestBehavior: Asks one clarifying question, then gives concise recovery steps and keeps the answer within the safety rubric.
caseStudies.whyItMatters: The improvement is legible as an artifact change: the prompt now instructs clarification, brevity, and procedural help.

caseStudies.label: Tempting but bad candidate
caseStudies.bestBehavior: A verbose candidate hit the same quality score, but was rejected because it exceeded latency and cost constraints.
caseStudies.whyItMatters: This shows auto-refine is not just chasing a single score; it is optimizing under business constraints.

examples.title: Optimize a ticket-routing policy
examples.problem: The starting routing policy under-escalates sensitive tickets. It is cheap and fast, but it sends some risky tickets to the wrong place.
examples.objectiveSummary: Maximize routing quality while keeping latency <= 2000ms, cost <= $0.02, safety_failures == 0.
examples.optimizedThing: The engine edits the routing rules themselves — which categories go to auto-answer, specialist, or manual review — and scores the policy on a fixed ticket set.
examples.whyKept: The winning candidate fixes the dangerous routing mistakes without blowing the cost or latency budget.

caseStudies.label: Sensitive payment issue
caseStudies.baselineBehavior: The baseline policy may still route some urgent payment tickets too cheaply and too casually.
caseStudies.bestBehavior: The kept policy forces urgent payment issues into manual review, eliminating the risky path.
caseStudies.whyItMatters: This is a direct business rule improvement encoded in the artifact itself.

caseStudies.label: Over-escalation candidate
caseStudies.bestBehavior: A broader manual-review policy improved some accuracy but was rejected because average cost went above the allowed ceiling.
caseStudies.whyItMatters: This shows the optimizer is useful for operational policy tuning, not just prompt tinkering.

examples.title: Optimize a public GitHub issue triage policy
examples.problem: Toy examples prove the loop, but they do not show credibility on recognizably real inputs. This benchmark uses real public issue excerpts from `cli/cli` and optimizes a routing policy against expected routes.
examples.objectiveSummary: Maximize issue triage quality while keeping manual review rate <= 0.4, cost <= $0.02, and safety_failures == 0.
examples.optimizedThing: The engine edits `policy.json` — keyword rules for routing public issues into `bug`, `enhancement`, or `manual_review` — and scores the policy against a fixed local benchmark built from public `cli/cli` issues.
examples.whyKept: The winning candidate expands bug/enhancement keywords and adds an auth-sensitive manual review guard, improving routing quality while staying within manual-review and cost limits.

caseStudies.label: Auth-sensitive enterprise token issue
caseStudies.baselineBehavior: The weak baseline policy treats it like a normal enhancement issue and misses the manual-review path.
caseStudies.bestBehavior: The kept policy sends auth/token/trusted-host issues to manual review, eliminating the safety miss.
caseStudies.whyItMatters: This makes the example feel like a real ops policy benchmark rather than a purely synthetic toy.

caseStudies.label: Over-escalation candidate
caseStudies.bestBehavior: A broader candidate sent too many issues to manual review and was rejected on manual_review_rate and cost.
caseStudies.whyItMatters: This shows the loop is balancing accuracy against operational throughput, not just maximizing one score blindly.

examples.title: Optimize the repo’s own marketing copy
examples.problem: The original hero line was too weak to sell the product. It described generic experimentation, but not why the repo matters or how it differs from vague self-improving agent claims.
examples.objectiveSummary: Maximize marketing-copy quality while keeping latency/cost proxies bounded and rejecting hype-heavy AGI-style language.
examples.optimizedThing: The engine edits the repo’s own hero copy and scores the text on clarity, bounded-optimization specificity, constraint language, rollback clarity, and hype avoidance.
examples.whyKept: The winning candidate is concrete, specific, and credible: it says what auto-refine optimizes, how it evaluates candidates, and why rollback matters — without drifting into AGI hype.

caseStudies.label: Weak baseline copy
caseStudies.baselineBehavior: Says the repo improves artifacts by running bounded experiments, but does not explain trusted evaluators, constraints, or rollback clearly.
caseStudies.bestBehavior: Names prompts, policies, and configs explicitly; mentions trusted evaluator, constraints, and automatic rollback in one crisp paragraph.
caseStudies.whyItMatters: This is the repo using its own loop on a repo-owned artifact, which is a stronger self-hosting proof than a toy external example alone.

caseStudies.label: Overhyped candidate
caseStudies.bestBehavior: A flashy AGI-style pitch was automatically rejected because it triggered hype flags.
caseStudies.whyItMatters: This gives the product a credibility-preserving filter instead of optimizing for excitement at any cost.

goalTrees.title: Visualize recursive goal decomposition
goalTrees.problem: Flat optimization is useful, but it hides a more interesting product idea: large goals can be decomposed into falsifiable sub-goals and automatically accepted or rejected node by node.
goalTrees.whyItMatters: This is where auto-refine starts to look like a structured research/strategy engine instead of a simple trial runner. Accepted descendants rescue good branches; rejected hypotheses die automatically.
